articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 23, 1912. History of the Church of the Crucifixion continued: rectors George Bringhurst (1856-1867, whose haughtiness drove off helpers like Miss Mary Adger), Joseph R. Moore (1867-1870, during whose tenure James W. Purnell was brutally assaulted en route to church), the West Indian William H. Josephus (1870-72, who also taught at the Institute for Colored Youth under Fanny M. Coppin), the listless Samuel Hazlehurst, and Rev. Henry L. Phillips from February 1877, whose long incumbency brought the Parish House (1888), McKean Street Mission (1891), rebuilding after the 1901 fire, the Workingmen's Club, Starr Savings Fund, Home for the Homeless, and custody of the American Negro Historical Society's records. Crucifixion was admitted to the Diocesan Convention in 1865 with Peter Williams and James Cornish as delegates. The Adger family (Mary, Lucy A., C. Samuel, William A., Hannah), the Chews, Lindsays, Sanders and Jacob Simmons were mainstays. Bolivar notes the long first-movement tenures of Richard Allen (1794-1831), Absalom Jones (1794-1818) and Rev. William Douglass (nearly thirty years at St. Thomas').

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 8, 1912. Panorama of Black Philadelphia in 1869: the Institute for Colored Youth under Fanny M. Jackson; Bird's, Vaux and Ohio Street schools under Messrs. Lawrence and White and Miss LeCount; the Freedmen's Bank at 919 Lombard Street with William Whipper as cashier; undertakers Duterte, LeCount, Allmond and Charnock; Rev. John B. Reeve at Central Presbyterian, T. Doughty Miller at Cherry Street Baptist, William J. Alston at St. Thomas'; Rev. Stephen Smith of Lombard Street, the richest colored man in the United States, with John McKee in the next square dying even wealthier; Liberty and Masonic Halls for dances and meetings; The Christian Recorder the only colored paper; the Banneker Institute in its sixteenth year led by Jacob C. White, Jr., Octavius V. Catto and St. George R. Taylor; Samuel R. Watts introducing bicycle riding; and the first cohort of medical students (John B. Bascom, Samuel K. Watts, Etienne C. Vidal, Samuel Frisby, Charles Shadd) finishing at Howard. David W. Brown in the Health Office was then the city government's only colored employee.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 1, 1914. Philadelphia's outsized share of Black American diplomats: Ebenezer D. Bassett, principal of the Institute for Colored Youth when appointed first Black U.S. Minister (to Haiti, 1869, under Grant); John H. Smythe, Minister to Liberia; John S. Durham, Consul to Santo Domingo and then Minister to Haiti; William F. Powell of Camden (a Bird's School pupil), Minister to Haiti, assisted by ICY graduate Charles L. Moore; Stanislas Gautier, a Philadelphian free lance in Haitian legations; Richard T. Greener, Consul at Vladivostok during the Russo-Japanese War; and Bishop William H. Heard, Minister to Liberia. Bolivar also recalls Daniel Murray's American Negro Historical Society lecture on the Black secretary of the French legation during Washington's administration.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 31, 1914. Philadelphia's part in Reconstruction: a score of Institute for Colored Youth graduates went south as teachers, and mustered-out soldiers stayed for preferment. Philadelphians and Pennsylvanians in southern office included Richard Gleaves, Lieutenant Governor of South Carolina; Jonathan C. Gibbs, Dartmouth graduate and former First African Presbyterian minister, in Florida; J. Henry Burch, sometime pastor of Shiloh Baptist, a Louisiana state senator; Alfred M. Green of the Christian Recorder in Mississippi; Theophilus J. Minton, Thaddeus K. Saportas and Stephen B. Gibson in South Carolina, with Chief Justice Wright and Senator Whipper also Pennsylvania natives; and John H. Smythe and John W. Cromwell, old ICY scholars, in Virginia. Rev. James Leach seconded Grant's 1872 renomination at the Academy of Music. Bolivar closes praising ex-Congressman John R. Lynch's newly published history of Reconstruction.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 3, 1912. Tribute to James Forten (d. 1842), 'the master-mind of his time': Revolutionary War service, sail-making fortune making him the richest colored man of his generation, a strong factor in the first insurance company (1796), active in one of the two initial church communions, one of three men who raised a brigade of colored soldiers in the War of 1812, prime mover of the first secular convention against colonization, financier whose contribution enabled Garrison to start The Liberator, and author of a remarkable address printed in the Women's Anti-Slavery Society publications of 1836. Reprints the Anti-Slavery Record tribute of December 1835 (signed 'W', likely Whittier) describing his sail loft where white and colored workmen labored together in harmony for more than twenty years, and the 1845 wealthy-citizens pamphlet crediting him with an envied estate of $100,000 — 'He was an honest man.' Joseph Cassey also appears in the 1845 list.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 20, 1912. Old Philadelphia street cries and markets: colored stallholders in the enclosed and curb markets (Walter P. Hall the last and most important); oyster and terrapin purveyors James LeCount, James Prosser and John Nixon stocking their bins before the Delaware froze; the colored monopoly of the ice cream trade; hot-corn women singing on the steps beside Independence Hall; Samuel Sorden crying his 'Callyloo'; the Dominican and Haitian peanut-cake sellers with white covered trays and bells; Emmons the whistling huckster; the pepper-pot and clam criers; and the first colored newsboy, a full-fledged man crying the Inquirer and Ledger during the Civil War.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 29, 1912. Review of R. R. Wright, Jr.'s new University of Pennsylvania dissertation 'The Negro in Pennsylvania': slavery in the colony (first discriminating enactment 1693, Penn himself a slave owner), the 1780 abolition act when 5,000 slaves remained in the state, Benezet's 1750 school whose pupils were all free-born, and the advocacy of Franklin, Dr. Benjamin Rush, Benjamin Lay and Anthony Benezet. Bolivar highlights Franklin's declared esteem for the intelligence of Samuel Bustill and notes that favorable white opinion arose only when the men of the Free African Society (1787) had demonstrated their moral, material and mental fitness. Much of Wright's data comes from Rev. William Douglass's 'Annals of St. Thomas'.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 3, 1914. Occasioned by criticism of France's Black soldiers in the European war, Bolivar recounts Daniel Murray's Library of Congress lecture before the American Negro Historical Society: Antoine Chaulette and Martial Besse named generals in the same 1795 French order that made Bonaparte a general; General Alexandre (Thomas) Dumas, father of the novelist, commander-in-chief of the Army of the Alps in 1793; Jean Baptiste Paschal, a Black man, arriving in 1793 as Secretary of the French Legation with Minister Genet — distasteful to President Washington but treated with marked consideration by Jefferson; and the near-riot in Philadelphia in 1798 when San Domingo deputies J. B. Belley and Jean Baptiste Mills landed en route to the French Convention. Bolivar also recalls the 1688 Germantown protest, Canada as the Underground Railroad's terminus, and Philadelphia's brigade and sailors of the War of 1812.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 12, 1912. Life of Rev. William Douglass: an A.M.E. minister who came to Philadelphia for the 1833 anti-slavery convention of colored men alongside John F. Cook and J. Fleet of Washington, he impressed James Needham and Robert Gordon in committee work. Dissatisfied with his communion and a believer in an educated ministry (even before Daniel A. Payne's like efforts), he married the daughter of Nicholas Grice — the man who had worked on the Genius of Universal Emancipation with Lundy and Garrison and first conceived the 1830 convention supported by Bishop Allen. With St. Thomas' Church without a rector, Douglass studied the ritual for a year, was ordained, and became rector in 1834, serving until his death soon after the opening of the Civil War. He lectured annually in the Institute for Colored Youth courses from 1853 under his close friend E. D. Bassett, sought an unapplied bequest in England in the early 1840s, and was the first Black American to publish a volume of sermons as well as the 'Annals of St. Thomas', the first history by a colored person based directly on records.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 16, 1912. Founding of the Church of the Crucifixion, on Rev. Henry L. Phillips's 36th anniversary as rector: Thomas A. Latimer, a layman of St. Paul's, began services in a room about 1846 among the very poor of Bedford, Lisle, Hubbell, Shippen and Emeline streets, where the Morris Brown A.M.E. Mission was the only church. Rev. Edward C. Jones became the first clerical worker in November 1846; Bishop Alonzo Potter sustained the movement; the church took the name Crucifixion in 1847 and was recognized in 1848 — though without diocesan representation, opponents like the pro-slavery Rev. Dr. Ducachet citing St. Thomas' (founded 1794 and still unavailingly pounding at the convention's door). A plain church seating 450 was built for $2,000 on Eighth above Bainbridge; Joseph Carter, a colored man, built its pipe organ and was its player. Members braved assaults from the rowdy, pro-slavery, Negro-hunting element a square below.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 25, 1914. Gleanings from surviving numbers of The Spectator, a Black Philadelphia paper of 1880-81: the Old Reliable Club ball (J. W. Purnell, A. W. Bettencourt, William Allen, P. A. Holmes); a Bethel Literary debate between A. G. Davis and Daniel P. Adger; William D. Forten and John D. Lewis addressing a Fifth Ward political club; the Tassonian Literary Society's officers (W. W. Still, Richard Taylor, Anna J. Cooper, Ida Somerville, Ella Williams); H. O. Tanner's early painting 'The Battle of Navarino' at the Workingmen's Club art show — the promising young artist a son of the Christian Recorder's editor, later a bishop; the death and funeral of Lucretia Mott (November 1880); a Crucifixion Sunday School concert managed by Rev. Henry L. Phillips with Fanny M. Jackson among participants; I. C. Wears and Levi Cromwell raising funds for William H. Chew's suit against the School Board; a reading by Frances E. W. Harper; and the marriage of W. H. Dorsey and Miss Olive Durham.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 17, 1914. On Gouldtown, New Jersey, the two-hundred-year-old settlement thirty miles below Philadelphia, occasioned by the new history from its sons William Steward and Rev. Theophilus G. Steward: descended from the Fenwicks of English Crown connection intermixed with Indian and African lines, its thousand-strong clan (with allied Pierce, Murray, Cuff and Steward centres) held its lands for two centuries. Bishop Richard Allen, Morris Brown and the woman preacher Jarena Lee helped shape its religious life, and before New Jersey had an A.M.E. conference Gouldtown was under the paternalism of Methodist Philadelphia, with pastorates including Rev. Jeremiah Miller (step-father of Robert Purvis), Paul Quinn (afterwards bishop), Jeremiah Beulah, J. A. Shorter, John Cornish, Clayton Durham, A. W. Wayman, Peter Gardner, Richard Barney, Joshua Woodlyn and Redman Fauset.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 18, 1913. Philadelphia's choral music history, prompted by Frank A. Clarke's Emancipation semi-centennial chorus: the first chorus conductor traced is John Cromwell, ninety-five years earlier (c. 1818); choral societies existed at St. Thomas' and First African Presbyterian as far back as 1835, trained by Morris Brown, Jr.; Brown organized a large chorus in 1840 which sang Haydn's 'Creation' in 1840 and 1841 with principals and an orchestra of fifty; the first break from lined-out singing at Bethel came in 1840 with a quartette of Mrs. Margaret Jones, Miss Elizabeth Clark, Hans Shadd and John Johnson — over protest from the rigid element still under the spell of Richard Allen; a printed programme evidences a St. Thomas' choir as far back as 1814. Later landmarks: Addison W. Lively and Franklin R. Guinn's Academy of Music chorus, the Amphion mixed chorus under John H. Bechtel (1874), and the Amphion male chorus under Samuel Adger (1881).

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, May 18, 1912. The four most conspicuous anti-slavery lecturers between 1831 and the 1863 Proclamation — Frederick Douglass, Henry Highland Garnet, William Wells Brown and Charles Lenox Remond, joined by Sojourner Truth and Frances E. W. Harper — frequently spoke in Philadelphia, the greatest centre of anti-slavery work, at the old Philadelphia Institute (Lombard above Seventh), Franklin Hall, Bethel, Wesley, the Seventh Street Presbyterian Church and National Hall. In the companion debates that educated men in the moral cause, Frederick Hinton, John C. Bowers and Isaiah C. Wears were the chief Philadelphia exponents, with Samuel R. Ward of New York an able second, forever combatting the prejudiced bookdealer John Campbell of 'Negro Mania'. Bolivar then surveys the elocutionists: Louise De Mortie, S. Morgan Smith, Malachi R. Dunmore, John H. Smyth, Richard T. Greener, Caroline R. LeCount, R. Henri Strange and Paul Laurence Dunbar.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 10, 1912. The Institute for Colored Youth in 1865-66: with the war's end and Lincoln's assassination there were no graduates in 1865, and the school instead held memorial literary exercises that May — the Alumni Association at Sansom Street Hall (addresses by Samuel C. Gould, James F. Needham on 'Obstacles to Progress', John F. Smith on 'The Character of Abraham Lincoln', and the Alumni Oration by Prof. William Howard Day) and the school at Concert Hall in the evening, with papers by Pliny I. Locke, Frazelia Campbell, T. J. Minton, Charles N. Thomas and others, a dialogue written by Sarah M. Douglass, and an original poem by Toussaint L'Ouverture Martin. Commencement resumed in 1866: an Alumni celebration (John Quincy Allen, Elizabeth Handy, a poem by Rebecca J. Cole, James LeCount, Jr.'s eulogium on Ellis Yarnall Tingle, and the oration by Rev. Dr. John B. Reeve) and school exercises at National Hall. It was the first year Fanny M. Jackson and Mary J. Patterson appeared on the programs.

events.name: William Douglass called to the rectorship of St. Thomas' Church
events.location: St. Thomas' African Episcopal Church, Philadelphia
events.description: William Douglass, an A.M.E. minister who came to Philadelphia as a delegate to the 1833 convention of colored men with John F. Cook and J. Fleet of Washington, was urged toward the vacant St. Thomas' rectorship by James Needham and Robert Gordon; after a year's study of the ritual and liturgy he was ordained and became rector, serving until his death soon after the opening of the Civil War (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 12, 1912).

events.name: Choral societies formed at St. Thomas' and First African Presbyterian
events.location: St. Thomas' and First African Presbyterian churches, Philadelphia
events.description: Choral societies connected with St. Thomas' and the First (African) Presbyterian churches existed as far back as 1835, trained by Morris Brown, Jr. (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 18, 1913).

events.name: Morris Brown Jr.'s chorus sings Haydn's 'Creation'
events.description: A large and ambitious chorus organized by Morris Brown, Jr. (son of Bishop Morris Brown) in 1840 sang the oratorio of 'The Creation' in 1840 and 1841, with principals and an orchestra of fifty (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 18, 1913).

events.name: First quartette choir at Bethel A.M.E. Church
events.description: The first break from lined-out singing at Bethel came in 1840 with a quartette of Mrs. Margaret Jones, Miss Elizabeth Clark, Hans Shadd and John Johnson; the innovation drew protest from the rigid element still under the spell of Richard Allen (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 18, 1913).

events.name: Anti-Slavery Record tribute to Forten's sail loft
events.description: The Anti-Slavery Record of December 1835 printed a visitor's tribute (signed 'W', likely Whittier) describing Forten's establishment of twenty to thirty workmen, about half colored, employed together in peace and harmony for more than twenty years — 'and who is this noble sail maker? He is a colored man!' (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 3, 1912).

events.name: Founding of the Church of the Crucifixion
events.description: Thomas A. Latimer, a layman of St. Paul's, began mission services about 1846 in a room among the very poor of Bedford, Lisle, Shippen and Emeline streets; Rev. Edward C. Jones became the first clerical worker in November 1846, Bishop Alonzo Potter sustained the work, the name Crucifixion was taken in 1847 and church recognition came in 1848 — without diocesan representation, over opposition from pro-slavery men like Rev. Dr. Ducachet. Joseph Carter, a colored man, built the pipe organ and was its player (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 16, 1912).

events.name: Anti-slavery debates in Philadelphia halls and churches
events.location: Philadelphia Institute, Franklin Hall, Bethel, Wesley, Seventh Street Presbyterian, National Hall
events.description: Debates were part of the scheme to educate men in the moral cause of abolition, of which Garrison was the ruling spirit; the chief Philadelphia exponents were Frederick Hinton, John C. Bowers and Isaiah C. Wears, with Samuel R. Ward of New York an able second, kept forever answering the bookdealer John Campbell of 'Negro Mania' (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, May 18, 1912).

events.name: Crucifixion Church admitted to the Diocesan Convention
events.description: After long years of appeal, the Church of the Crucifixion became a member of the Protestant Episcopal Diocesan Convention in 1865, its delegates being Peter Williams and James Cornish, both zealous workers and intelligent men (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 23, 1912).

events.name: ICY memorial literary exercises in lieu of commencement
events.location: Sansom Street Hall and Concert Hall, Philadelphia
events.description: With no graduates in 1865 owing to the war and Lincoln's assassination, the Institute for Colored Youth held literary exercises that May: the Alumni Association at Sansom Street Hall with addresses by Samuel C. Gould, James F. Needham and John F. Smith and the Alumni Oration by Prof. William Howard Day; the school at Concert Hall in the evening with student papers, a dialogue written by Sarah M. Douglass and an original poem by Toussaint L'Ouverture Martin (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 10, 1912).

events.name: ICY commencement exercises resumed
events.location: ICY school building and National Hall, Philadelphia
events.description: In 1866 the Institute for Colored Youth resumed its old manner of commencement: an Alumni Celebration in the school building (John Quincy Allen's introductory remarks, Elizabeth Handy on music, a poem by Rebecca J. Cole, James LeCount, Jr.'s eulogium on Ellis Yarnall Tingle, and the Alumni Oration by Rev. Dr. John B. Reeve) and regular school exercises at National Hall, Market below Thirteenth; the first year Fanny M. Jackson and Mary J. Patterson appeared on the programs (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 10, 1912).

events.name: Freedmen's Savings Bank branch at 919 Lombard Street
events.description: In 1869 the Freedmen's Bank operated at 919 Lombard Street with William Whipper as cashier, doing a flourishing business (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 8, 1912).
